title: More Vectorized Comparisons and Logical Subsetting
description: Deep dive into logical operations and subsetting with conditions.

slides.title: Comparison Operators 🔍
slides.content:
Comparison operators are vectorized and return logical vectors:

* `==` – equal to
* `!=` – not equal to
* `>` , `<` , `>=` , `<=`
* `%in%` – checks if elements are in another vector

```r
x <- 1:5
x == 3          # FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE FALSE
x %in% c(2,4,6) # FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE FALSE
```

slides.title: Comparison Examples
slides.codeSnippet:
x <- c(5, 8, 2, 9, 1)
print(x > 4)
print(x == 8)
print(x %in% c(2,9,10))

quizData.question: What does `c(1,2,3) %in% c(3,4,5)` return?
quizData.explanation: Only 3 is in the second set, so FALSE, FALSE, TRUE.

slides.title: Combining Conditions with Logical Operators
slides.content:
Use `&` (AND) and `|` (OR) to combine logical vectors. Remember they are vectorized, so they operate element-wise.

```r
x <- 1:10
x[(x > 3) & (x < 8)]   # 4 5 6 7
```

For scalar comparisons (if you only have one condition to check), you can use `&&` and `||`, but they are not vectorized.

slides.title: Complex Subsetting
slides.codeSnippet:
data <- c(12, 5, 8, 15, 3, 20)
# Select numbers that are either less than 10 or greater than 15
result <- data[(data < 10) | (data > 15)]
print(result)

# Select numbers that are between 5 and 15 inclusive
mid <- data[(data >= 5) & (data <= 15)]
print(mid)

slides.title: any() and all()
slides.content:
`any()` returns TRUE if at least one element is TRUE; `all()` returns TRUE if all are TRUE.

```r
x <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
any(x)   # TRUE
all(x)   # FALSE
```

These are great for checking conditions:

```r
any(x > 10)   # check if any value exceeds 10
all(x > 0)    # check if all values are positive
```

quizData.question: Given `v <- c(2,4,6,8)`, what does `all(v %% 2 == 0)` return?
quizData.explanation: All elements are even, so all are TRUE, thus `all()` returns TRUE.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
Challenging Subsetting
slides.content: Create a vector `nums <- c(3, 8, 2, 5, 9, 1, 7, 4)`. Extract all numbers that are either less than 4 or greater than 7. Then check if any number is exactly 10.
slides.codeSnippet:
nums <- c(3, 8, 2, 5, 9, 1, 7, 4)
selected <- nums[(nums < 4) | (nums > 7)]
print(selected)
print(any(nums == 10))
